instruction: Explain this in simple language for a citizen: Can WhatsApp chats be used as evidence?
input: Electronic records can be relevant evidence, but authenticity and proper proof matter. Section 65B of the old Evidence Act remains a common reference point in legal practice for certification issues, and the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam framework still focuses on authenticity, source, and reliability of electronic records.
output:
Short answer: WhatsApp chats can be relevant evidence, but they should be preserved carefully. Keep the original device, export the chats if possible, and retain timestamps and surrounding messages so the record is not taken out of context. Certification or other proof requirements may still matter depending on how the record is used in court.

Why it matters: Electronic records can be relevant evidence, but authenticity and proper proof matter.

Most relevant sections:
- Section 65B of the old Evidence Act remains a common reference point in legal practice for certification issues

Practical next steps:
- Keep the original device, export the chats or files, and preserve timestamps.
- Do not edit or crop the material in a way that hides context or metadata.
- Ask for legal review if admissibility or certification of the record will matter.

Grounded citations: Section 65B of the old Evidence Act remains a common reference point in legal practice for certification issues.

instruction: Explain Act No. 47 of 2023 | Section 93 in plain language.
input: 93. Presumption as to electronic records five years old . - Where any electronic record, purporting or proved to be five years old, is produced from any custody which the Court in the particular case considers proper, the Court may presume that the electronic signature which purports to be the electronic signature of any particular person was so affixed by him or any person authorised by him in this behalf. Explanation. - The Explanation to section 81 shall also apply to this section. CHAPTER VI OF THE EXCLUSION OF ORAL EVIDENCE BY DOCUMENTARY EVIDENCE
output:

instruction: Explain Act No. 45 of 2023 | Section 262 in plain language.
input: 262. Resistance or obstruction by a person to his lawful apprehension . - Whoever intentionally offers any resistance or illegal obstruction to the lawful apprehension of himself for any offence with which he is charged or of which he has been convicted, or escapes or attempts to escape from any custody in which he is lawfully detained for any such offence, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both. Explanation. - The punishment in this section is in addition to the punishment for which the person to be apprehended or detained in custody was liable for the offence with which he was charged, or of which he was convicted.
output:
Short answer: Resistance or obstruction by a person to his lawful apprehension . means whoever intentionally offers any resistance or illegal obstruction to the lawful apprehension of himself for any offence with which he is charged or of which he has been convicted, or escapes or attempts to escape from any custody in which he is lawfully detained for any such off.

Why it matters: Use Act No. 45 of 2023 | Section 262 when the facts match this rule and avoid stretching it beyond the actual ingredients of the section.

Most relevant sections:
- Act No. 45 of 2023 | Section 262

Practical next steps:

- Preserve the original documents, screenshots, messages, and witness details.
- Get lawyer review quickly if the issue could lead to police action or court process.

Grounded citations: Act No. 45 of 2023 | Section 262.

input: Act No. 22 of 2023 | Section 4 deals with the grounds for processing personal data under the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023. Act No. 22 of 2023 | Section 8 deals with the general obligations of the Data Fiduciary. Together they reflect that processing must rest on a valid legal basis and that the entity handling data has compliance obligations.
output:
Short answer: The DPDP Act 2023 is built around lawful processing and obligations on the entity handling personal data. In simple terms, data cannot be used casually just because it was collected; the legal basis, purpose, and compliance duties still matter.

instruction: Explain Act No. 45 of 2023 | Section 350 in plain language.
input: 350. Making a false mark upon any receptacle containing goods . - (1) Whoever makes any false mark upon any case, package or other receptacle containing goods, in a manner reasonably calculated to cause any public servant or any other person to believe that such receptacle contains goods which it does not contain or that it does not contain goods which it does contain, or that the goods contained in such receptacle are of a nature or quality different from the real nature or quality thereof, shall, unless he proves that he acted without intent to defraud, be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both. (2) Whoever makes use of any false mark in any manner prohibited under sub -section ( 1) shall, unless he proves that he acted without intent to defraud, be punished as if he had committed the offence under sub-section (1). CHAPTER XIX OF CRIMINAL INTIMIDATION, INSULT, ANNOYANCE, DEFAMATION, ETC.
output:

instruction: Explain Act No. 45 of 2023 | Section 294 in plain language.
input: 294. Sale, etc., of obscene books, etc . - (1) For the purposes of sub -section (2), a book, pamphlet, paper, writing, drawing, painting, representation, figure or any other object, including display of any content in electronic form shall be deemed to be obscene if it is lascivious or appeals to the prurient interest or if its effect, or (where it comprises two or more distinct items) the effect of any one of its items, is, if taken as a whole, such as to tend to deprave and corrupt persons who are likely, having regard to all relevant circumstances, to read, see or hear the matter contained or embodied in it. instruction: Explain this in simple language for a citizen: What is the punishment for cheating under BNS?
input: Act No. 45 of 2023 | Section 318(2) says simple cheating may be punished with imprisonment up to three years, fine, or both. Act No. 45 of 2023 | Section 318(3) increases the punishment up to five years where the cheating causes wrongful loss to a person whose interest the accused was bound to protect. Act No. 45 of 2023 | Section 318(4) provides punishment up to seven years where cheating dishonestly induces delivery of property or alteration or destruction of valuable security.
output:
Short answer: The punishment for cheating under BNS depends on the facts. Simple cheating can attract up to three years' imprisonment, fine, or both. More serious forms can go up to five years or seven years, especially where the deception causes wrongful loss or leads to delivery of property or valuable security.

Why it matters: Act No. 45 of 2023 | Section 318(2) says simple cheating may be punished with imprisonment up to three years, fine, or both.

Most relevant sections:
- Act No. 45 of 2023 | Section 318(2)
- Act No. 45 of 2023 | Section 318(3)
- Act No. 45 of 2023 | Section 318(4)

Practical next steps:
- Preserve transaction IDs, bank alerts, payment screenshots, and chat records.
- Write down the exact timeline, account numbers, phone numbers, and platform details.
- Report quickly to the bank, platform, and police because delay can hurt recovery options.

Grounded citations: Act No. 45 of 2023 | Section 318(2), Act No. 45 of 2023 | Section 318(3), Act No. 45 of 2023 | Section 318(4).

instruction: Explain this in simple language for a citizen: What are my rights if I am arrested in India?
input: Act No. 46 of 2023 | Section 47 says the arrested person must be told the grounds of arrest and, in bailable cases, the right to bail. Act No. 46 of 2023 | Section 38 says an arrested person may meet an advocate of his choice during interrogation, though not throughout it.
output:
Short answer: If you are arrested, you should be told why you are being arrested. In bailable matters, you should also be informed that you can seek bail. You can ask to meet a lawyer during interrogation, and you should keep copies of the arrest and bail paperwork.

Why it matters: Act No. 46 of 2023 | Section 47 says the arrested person must be told the grounds of arrest and, in bailable cases, the right to bail.

instruction: Explain Act No. 47 of 2023 | Section 126 in plain language.
input: 126. Competency of husband and wife as witnesses in certain cases . - (1) In all civil proceedings the parties to the suit, and the husband or wife of any party to the suit, shall be competent witnesses. (2) In criminal proceedings against any person, the husband or wife of such person, respectively, shall be a competent witness.

instruction: Explain Act No. 45 of 2023 | Section 22 in plain language.
input: 22. Act of a person of unsound mind. - Nothing is an offence which is done by a person who, at the time of doing it, by reason of unsoundness of mind, is incapable of knowing the nature of the act, or that he is doing what is either wrong or contrary to law.

Short answer: Act of a person of unsound mind. means nothing is an offence which is done by a person who, at the time of doing it, by reason of unsoundness of mind, is incapable of knowing the nature of the act, or that he is doing what is either wrong or contrary to law.

instruction: Explain Act No. 45 of 2023 | Section 67 in plain language.
input: 67. Sexual intercourse by husband upon his wife during separation . - Whoever has sexual intercourse with his own wife, who is living separately, whether under a decree of separation or otherwise, without her consent, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which shall not be less than two years but which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine. Explanation. - In this section, "sexual intercourse " shall mean any of the acts mentioned in clauses (a) to (d) of section 63.
output:

instruction: Answer this using the exact section names from the context and keep it easy to understand: What are my rights if I am arrested in India?
input: Act No. 46 of 2023 | Section 47 says the arrested person must be told the grounds of arrest and, in bailable cases, the right to bail. Act No. 46 of 2023 | Section 38 says an arrested person may meet an advocate of his choice during interrogation, though not throughout it.
output:
Short answer: If you are arrested, you should be told why you are being arrested. In bailable matters, you should also be informed that you can seek bail. You can ask to meet a lawyer during interrogation, and you should keep copies of the arrest and bail paperwork.

Why it matters: Act No. 46 of 2023 | Section 47 says the arrested person must be told the grounds of arrest and, in bailable cases, the right to bail.

Most relevant sections:
- Act No. 46 of 2023 | Section 47
- Act No. 46 of 2023 | Section 38

Practical next steps:
- Ask for the grounds of arrest and keep a copy of any arrest or bail paperwork.
- Inform a family member or trusted person and contact a lawyer quickly.
- Record the time, place, officer details, and any medical issues without delay.

Grounded citations: Act No. 46 of 2023 | Section 47, Act No. 46 of 2023 | Section 38.

instruction: Give a short answer, list the most relevant sections, and add practical next steps: What is the role of CrPC Section 156 after an FIR is recorded?
input: Act No. 2 of 1974 | Section 156 deals with the police officer's power to investigate cognizable cases. Once the matter is within that category, the police can proceed with investigation according to law, subject to judicial oversight in later stages.
output:
Short answer: CrPC Section 156 deals with investigation after the cognizable case threshold is crossed. It matters because recording the complaint is only the first step; investigation, evidence collection, and later reporting still have to happen lawfully.

Why it matters: Act No. 2 of 1974 | Section 156 deals with the police officer's power to investigate cognizable cases.

Most relevant sections:
- Act No. 2 of 1974 | Section 156

Practical next steps:
- Write down the facts in date-wise order and keep copies of every complaint you submit.
- Preserve the refusal details, station name, officer details, and any receiving copy or email proof.
- Escalate quickly to senior police officers or a Magistrate if the matter is urgent.

Grounded citations: Act No. 2 of 1974 | Section 156.

instruction: Explain Act No. 45 of 2023 | Section 278 in plain language.
input: 278. Sale of drug as a different drug or preparation . - Whoever knowingly sells, or offers or exposes for sale, or issues from a dispensary for medicinal purposes, any drug or medical preparation, as a different drug or medical preparation, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both.

instruction: Explain Act No. 47 of 2023 | Section 128 in plain language.
input: 128. Communications during marriage . - No person who is or has been married, shall be compelled to disclose any communication made to him during marriage by any person to whom he is or has been married; nor shall he be permitted to disclose any such communication, unless the person who made it, or his representative in interest, consents, except in suits between married persons, or proceedings in which one married person is prosecuted for any crime committed against the other.
output:

instruction: A user asks: Can the police refuse to register an FIR for a cognizable offence? Give a grounded answer with practical next steps.
input: Act No. 46 of 2023 | Section 173 deals with information relating to a cognizable offence. Act No. 46 of 2023 | Section 173(3) allows a limited preliminary enquiry in certain cases punishable for three years or more but less than seven years to check whether a prima facie case exists.
output:
Short answer: For a cognizable offence, the police are expected to record the information and act according to law rather than dismiss it casually. A limited preliminary enquiry may happen in some cases, but that is not the same as refusing the complaint altogether. Keep proof of submission and escalate quickly if the police station does not act.

Why it matters: Act No. 46 of 2023 | Section 173 deals with information relating to a cognizable offence.

instruction: What is the punishment for cheating under BNS?
input: Act No. 45 of 2023 | Section 318(2) says simple cheating may be punished with imprisonment up to three years, fine, or both. Act No. 45 of 2023 | Section 318(3) increases the punishment up to five years where the cheating causes wrongful loss to a person whose interest the accused was bound to protect. Act No. 45 of 2023 | Section 318(4) provides punishment up to seven years where cheating dishonestly induces delivery of property or alteration or destruction of valuable security.
output:
Short answer: The punishment for cheating under BNS depends on the facts. Simple cheating can attract up to three years' imprisonment, fine, or both. More serious forms can go up to five years or seven years, especially where the deception causes wrongful loss or leads to delivery of property or valuable security.

Why it matters: Act No. 45 of 2023 | Section 318(2) says simple cheating may be punished with imprisonment up to three years, fine, or both.

Most relevant sections:
- Act No. 45 of 2023 | Section 318(2)
- Act No. 45 of 2023 | Section 318(3)
- Act No. 45 of 2023 | Section 318(4)

Practical next steps:
- Preserve transaction IDs, bank alerts, payment screenshots, and chat records.
- Write down the exact timeline, account numbers, phone numbers, and platform details.
- Report quickly to the bank, platform, and police because delay can hurt recovery options.

Grounded citations: Act No. 45 of 2023 | Section 318(2), Act No. 45 of 2023 | Section 318(3), Act No. 45 of 2023 | Section 318(4).

instruction: Explain Act No. 45 of 2023 | Section 257 in plain language.
input: 257. Public servant in judicial proceeding corruptly making report, etc., contrary to law . - Whoever, being a public servant, corruptly or maliciously makes or pronounces in any stage of a judicial proceeding, any report, order, verdict, or decision which he knows to be contrary to law, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, or with fine, or with both.
output:
Short answer: Public servant in judicial proceeding corruptly making report, etc., contrary to law . means whoever, being a public servant, corruptly or maliciously makes or pronounces in any stage of a judicial proceeding, any report, order, verdict, or decision which he knows to be contrary to law, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend.
